text: the Clinic’s entrance. There are medical personnel inside, and more inbound. Whether you’re a bot or a human, if you are injured, please proceed to the Clinic for treatment .” He approached Ronin and Lara as the crowd thinned. Bots and humans alike assisted the wounded in a slow, careful journey to the Clinic, where soldiers were quickly clearing away rubble and deactivated gearheads . “Heartwarming as this is, you two need medical attention,” Cooper said, smiling. Maul and Chester came up behind him, the latter with several new holes in the front of his casing. His walk was labored, but he stood steady . “We’ll get to it,” Lara said . “Thank you.” Ronin nodded to Chester. “Thank you for keeping her safe. We owe you everything .” Chester grinned. “All I did was stand in front of some bullets. Not the hardest job I’ve had .” Lara chuckled. “I would’ve done the same for you, except, you know. The death thing .” Ronin laughed, though he didn’t find the joke particularly funny. She was alive . And so was he . Epilogue R onin walked along the sidewalk, optics roaming over the expanse of open land across the street. As many times as he’d seen the park in the forty-six years since coming to Cheyenne, he never tired of it — the place was ever-changing. For the last few decades, most of it had been repurposed for farming. Coupled with the fields outside of town, Cheyenne had a food surplus that attracted traders from miles around . Children ran and played between rows of crops, laughing and smiling, especially when fieldworkers shooed them away. Ronin knew them all by name, knew which of them were likely to leave signs on Mr. Mather’s back. The teacher still hadn’t caught on to their tricks . “ Dad !” Stopping, Ronin turned. Tabitha jogged toward him, a bundle of red flowers in her hand. Ronin’s memory flashed back to the first time he’d held his daughter. Tabitha had been so tiny and fragile, small enough to hold in one hand. It hadn’t mattered that her biological father was someone else; Ronin bonded with her instantly . Now, Tabitha was a grown woman with children of her own. She possessed so many of Lara’s features — the red hair, the bright blue eyes, the stubborn streak. The thirty-six years since her birth had gone by quickly, though they’d been full of joy and meaning . Tabitha grinned as she reached him. “Found these on my way back from the fields. I thought mom would like them .” Ronin smiled, too, and accepted the flowers. “She’ll love them .” “How’s she been? I visited with the kids yesterday, but I think they tired her out pretty quick .” Ronin cupped Tabitha’s cheek with his free hand, brushing a thumb over her light dusting of freckles. “She tires easily. Not that those kids don’t tire me out , too .” She chuckled. “Tell me about it. I gotta run — Dan’s waiting for me. We’re going to do some repairs on the wall. Let mom know I’ll stop by tomorrow. The kids have school and it’s my day off, so I can help her around the house if she needs it .” “I’ll let her know .” Tabitha stepped forward and wrapped her arms around him, kissing his cheek. “Love you guys .” He hugged her back and kissed her hair, as he had throughout her youth. “Love you too, Tabby .” Ronin watched her go. Had he made the right decision? Tabitha wasn’t a child anymore. She knew Lara’s health was waning, knew that, despite all the comforts, death was a reality in Cheyenne. She’d been old enough to understand what was happening during the famine, and was nearly twenty when sickness swept through the town and killed dozens . But he couldn’t’ bring himself to say anything that would kill the joy always sparkling in her eyes . Finally, he resumed his walk, greeting the neighbors who were tending their gardens. Flowers had become popular in recent years, but most folk still grew their own crops — the struggles of the past were not forgotten here, though so many of the people who had suffered under Warlord and risen up against him were gone. Their legacies continued, carried by their children and grandchildren, new generations who worked alongside bots to build a prosperous community . He opened the front gate and entered the yard, closing it gently behind him. Sometimes it was still strange, coming home like this, especially with his memories of the time before. Once, this had just been a place to store and care for his gear. A temporary stop between long journeys into the Dust. A building devoid of life and largely empty of purpose . But Lara had made it a home . Ronin climbed the steps onto the porch. Chimes — crafted from scrap metal Lara had collected over the years — tinkled quietly in the breeze. The same sound that had drawn him to her, nearly half a century ago. The lure of fate . He stepped inside and called her name as he shut the door behind him. The living room didn’t have a single patch of bare wall; Lara had remained a collector, as she dubbed herself, throughout her life. Decades-worth of trinkets adorned the walls and numerous shelves, displayed proudly for any guests . “In here,” she said . Ronin followed her voice toward the kitchen, passing the seashells she’d gathered when they visited the western ocean. There was a jar of sand from the same trip on one of the end tables, a heart-shaped rock pressed against the glass. Ronin hadn’t understood the need to bring more sand back to a town already full of dust and dirt, but he had carried it nonetheless . Lara stood at the counter, smearing jam onto a slice of bread.
